question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the amount the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has requested from leaseholders at Rossmore Green, Greenisland, for improvement and maintenance schemes in the last 12 months; (ii) what consultation took place with leaseholders; and (iii) to outline the rationale for these schemes.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed that it has not requested any monies from leaseholders at Rossmore Green, Greenisland for improvement and maintenance schemes within the last 12 months.  However four leaseholders have been provided with a Statement of planned scheme works (estimate) costs for works due to be carried out to the premises in which their sold flats are situated, the total estimated costs being £67,915.70.  These estimated costs are advised at least one month prior to commencement of the scheme works to the blocks where sold flats are situated.The Housing Executive has confirmed that a letter was sent to leaseholders on 12th June 2019 advising that a contractor had been appointed to carry out a condition survey to help finalise the work to be included in the scheme. It also stated that leaseholders would be responsible for the costs as per their Lease and they would be advised of the estimated costs in the future. A further letter of estimated costs was then issued to leaseholders on the 17th June 2022.The Greenisland Balconies scheme was identified following a specific survey to detail proposed remedial works to address issues with damp following complaints by both tenants and leaseholders. Works required include replacement of defective cavity wall insulation, damp penetration around fire escape balconies and defective window surrounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the amount the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has requested from leaseholders at Glassillan Grove, Greenisland, for improvement and maintenance schemes in the last 12 months; (ii) what consultation took place with leaseholders; and (iii) to outline the rationale for these schemes.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has confirmed that it has not requested any monies from leaseholders at Glassillan Grove, Greenisland for improvement and maintenance schemes within the last 12 months.However the Housing Executive has confirmed that 17 leaseholders in Glassillan Grove, Greenisland, have been provided with a Statement of planned scheme works (estimate) costs for works due to be carried out to the building/premises in which their sold flats are situated.  The total estimated costs being £353,660.48.  These estimated costs are advised at least one month prior to commencement of the scheme works to the blocks where sold flats are situated.A letter was sent to leaseholders on 12th June 2019 advising that a contractor had been appointed to carry out a condition survey to help finalise the work to be included in the scheme. It also stated that leaseholders would be responsible for the costs as per the conditions of their lease, and that they would be advised of the estimated costs in the future. A further letter of estimated costs was then issued to leaseholders on the 17th June 2022.The scheme Greenisland Balconies 22/70/1001 was identified by the Housing Executive’s District Office who requested a specific survey of the dwellings to provide a list of proposed remedial works to address issues with damp in the 76 dwellings following complaints by both tenants and leaseholders. Works required includes replacement of defective cavity wall insulation, damp penetration around fire escape balconies and defective window surrounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a bond was ever attached to the land at Cloughlands Park, Carrickfergus, BT38 7NJ.
answer_text: My officials have advised that the part of Cloughlands Park to which you refer is an unadopted private road which has never been subject to a Planning Approval or Private Streets Agreement, therefore no bond of surety would exist.  I understand that a planning approval for the recently constructed houses at Cloughlands Park required a developer to upgrade part of the existing private road to adoption standard.  This section of Cloughlands Park is now adopted and part of the road network which is maintained by my Department.  However, this adoption only relates to the entrance road and the first cul-de-sac.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps her Department is taking to promote Carrickfergus Castle as part of Northern Ireland’s tourism product.
answer_text: Carrickfergus Castle is currently operated as a heritage tourism attraction.  The Castle is regularly promoted and up to date information on opening hours etc. is available on www.discovernorthernireland.com .

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 854/22-27, what steps will his Department take to rectify the low levels of funding offers for businesses from Invest NI in East Antrim, in comparison to other parliamentary constituency areas.
answer_text: Invest NI provides a range of financial and practical support to help new business start-up and business growth across all of Northern Ireland including the East Antrim parliamentary area.  Over the period 2016-2021 Invest NI made 352 offers of support to businesses in the East Antrim Parliamentary Constituency area consisting of £10m of financial assistance, leveraging total investment of £47m. In response to the pandemic, additional financial support of £6.3m support was delivered through the various COVID schemes to 826 applicants in the Parliamentary Constituency area.The nature and level of support offered to businesses is demand driven as opposed to specific geographic locations. We are working hard with partners to increase demand for our business support in the  East Antrim area. To do this will require a higher level of growth projects coming forward to Invest NI in the region from established businesses and indeed higher levels of new business start- ups with export potential.  As with recovery following the pandemic, a collaborative delivery approach is essential, with all economic development partners, including Council, Chambers of Commerce and private sector partners.Invest NI’s North East Regional Office has been actively working to increase demand for support with the key economic development stakeholders in the area. A specific partnership initiative to encourage local businesses to consider exporting for the first time was recently completed. Under the theme ‘The Export Journey’, six monthly webinars were delivered between October ’21 & March ’22 with support from Invest NI’s Export Division. Mid and East Antrim Council led on this initiative. Other partners included the local Enterprise Agencies, Regional College, and Chambers of Commerce. In addition to the monthly webinars, 1:1 follow on support was provided to 68 local businesses. Similar partnership initiatives are in progress in the area to encourage higher levels of innovation amongst local businesses as well as the promotion of self-employment as a career option.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 855/22-27, what steps will his Department be taking to rectify the low number of jobs created with Invest NI support in East Antrim, in comparison to other parliamentary constituency areas.
answer_text: Over the period 2016-2021 Invest NI support has assisted with the creation of 194 jobs in the East Antrim Parliamentary Constituency area. In partnership with the local Council a further 500 jobs have been promoted across the Council area via the Northern Ireland Business Start Up Programme and wider Local Economic Development programmes delivered via the Local Economic Development (LED) measure.  As I highlighted in answer to an earlier question, (AQW 011961), the level of support offered by Invest NI to assist with business growth, and subsequent job creation, is demand driven as opposed to specific geographic locations. My department and Invest NI are working hard with partners to increase demand for our business support in East Antrim, and indeed the wider Mid and East Antrim Council area. This requires a higher level of growth projects, with new jobs therein, coming forward to Invest NI from established businesses in the region and indeed higher levels of new business start-ups with export potential.  Internationally, Invest NI promotes Northern Ireland as an attractive and viable location for inward investment opportunities to new investors, along with the parent companies of existing foreign owned investors. While we promote the merits of East Antrim as part of the wider NI proposition, ultimately the investor decides on the final location based on their specific business needs and having considered the options available to them.Job creation comes via a number of means (Private and Public sectors). The business sector make up of an area can also quite often be a determining factor. This has necessitated a higher level of collaboration and partnership working than in the past to deliver business growth and subsequent job creation. In addition to being Statutory Partners in the delivery of Community Plans, in place for all 11 Councils, Invest NI will continue ongoing close co-operation with all economic development partners in the Mid and East Antrim area to deliver the outcomes targeted in their plan, including job creation. The Belfast ‘City Deal’, which includes a number of potential projects to be delivered in the Mid and East Antrim area, provides a significant opportunity for economic growth and future job creation. Alongside partners, Invest NI will play a key role in ensuring that these deals are commercially focused and bring economic benefit to the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many (i) members of staff are required to fully staff Maghaberry Prison; (ii) members of staff currently work at Maghaberry Prison; and (iii) posts remain vacant at Maghaberry Prison.
answer_text: HMP Maghaberry is a high security facility and as such it would not be appropriate to outline the number of staff required to operate the prison; the number of staff currently working at the prison; or the number of posts that are currently vacant. To do so could impact upon the safe and secure operation of the prison.The Director General is committed to ensuring that staffing levels across the Service are maintained and to that end a number of recruitment competitions are currently ongoing.  Overall the Northern Ireland Prison Service is currently managing a significant increase in the prison population and a higher than normal level of staff absent due to a combination of sickness absence and absence as a result of COVID-19.  As a result Governors are required to undertake daily dynamic risk assessments which include reviewing staffing levels, ensuring essential services are delivered and assessing the level of regime that can be provided to facilitate the rehabilitation of those in our care.  Such assessments allow the Governor to make balanced decisions in relation to staff deployment, the level of regime that can be offered and what activities and services can be delivered each day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether prisoners are tested for drugs before being temporarily released.
answer_text: Prisoners are tested for drugs at various stages throughout their time in custody.  It is an offence against prison discipline for a prisoner to refuse to provide a sample when required to do so for drug testing purposes. These tests may be undertaken randomly, upon receipt of information or intelligence, or as a result of reasonable suspicion that a prisoner has taken unauthorised substances.  Tests may also be required prior to any periods of pre-release temporary leave subject to a risk assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the rollout of the £500 Staff Recognition Payment for Independent Care Home employees who worked during the qualifying period but have since left their employer.
answer_text: Phase 2 had been temporarily paused to allow time to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when all relevant information is provided by the claimants. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I can confirm that as at 16th June 2022, a total of over £16.67m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,470 individuals. As at the same date over £4.22m has been paid out to 9,857 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £419k has been paid. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. I can confirm that on the 17th June 2022 my officials launched an online portal which will allow those in the independent sector whose employers have ceased trading to apply for the Special Recognition Payment.Staff who worked in the independent sector (care home/domiciliary care/supported living) whose employer is no longer trading are eligible to apply for this payment if they were employed for a continuous 4 week period between 17 March 2020 and 31 January 2021. Applications can be made online through the following link: https://www.smartsurvey.co.uk/s/SRPnolongertrading/ A dedicated helpline is also available from 10am – 12.30pm Monday to Friday. The helpline number is: 0800 170 13 50. In addition, a full list of Frequently Asked Questions is available through the link below online for those in the independent sector whose employers have ceased trading: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/special-recognition-payments-former-employees-where-provider-has-ceased-trading A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to improve access to GP services in East Antrim.
answer_text: The Department of Health has invested significant funding within General Practice in order to help GP practices adapt to the challenges of the ongoing Covid-19 pandemic and meet the high demand for their services.    Regionally, funding of up to £5.5million was made available to support General Practice in Northern Ireland over the winter period and the sixteen GP Practices in the East Antrim area would have received their fair share of that investment. This included up to £1.7million to further improve telephony infrastructure and accessibility, in addition to £1.7million already invested to upgrade GP telephony.Investment and support will continue to be provided, including but not limited to, an increase in the number of NI GP training places in 2022/23 and also the continuing roll out of Multidisciplinary Teams within GP practices (not yet available in the East Antrim locality) which will help to alleviate a number of the issues currently being experienced across general practice, particularly in relation to high demand for GP services.Staff from my Department are currently completing a round of visits to all GP practices in Northern Ireland discussing each practice’s self-assessment of staffing capacity, service pressures and suggestions for improvement. Aspects of access including arrangements for the registration of new patients; the approach to infection prevention and control measures in line with public health guidance; and access for those with disabilities are all covered in these visits. The practice’s compliance with the General Medical Services (NI) regulations is discussed including those elements relevant to access.  Access to integrated care for those with multiple morbidities is important so for example, GP practices in East Antrim can refer older people to the Mid and East Antrim Agewell Partnership, an alliance between Health and Social Care, the Mid and East Antrim Council and the voluntary sector which on referral from the GP provides access to social support and activities for those aged over 60 who may be suffering from loneliness, bereavement or the trauma of disease onset in later life. Access to all healthcare services has and continues to be very challenging and East Antrim GP services are no exception. Improvements depend on the availability of funding, of workforce and the phased introduction of new technologies. Much has been achieved during a difficult period however there is still much to do.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the schools in East Antrim that are benefitting from the School Enhancement Programme.
answer_text: Four schools in East Antrim have been announced to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2).School NameCurrent StatusRoddensvale SchoolA design team has been appointed. The Technical Feasibility Report is complete and a Business Case is being developed. Thornfield House SchoolA design team has been appointed. The Technical Feasibility Report is complete and a Business Case is being developed. Carrickfergus Grammar SchoolA design team has been appointed. The Technical Feasibility Report is due for submission to the Education Authority by late summer 2022.Greenisland Primary SchoolA design team has been appointed. The Technical Feasibility Report is due for submission to the Education Authority by late summer 2022.
